behaviors.description: Conversation history is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when it exceeds the context window, ensuring the LLM receives properly scoped context
behaviors.justification: A unit test of the trim function cannot catch the failure where trimmed context is computed but never passed to the LLM. We must verify at integration level that the LLM call receives the trimmed list as a parameter, not the full history.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM API call. Invoke the chatbot with a conversation history exceeding 10 messages. Assert the mocked LLM was invoked with exactly the 10 most recent messages in the context parameter, not the full history.

behaviors.description: The generated response directly addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply
behaviors.justification: This failure depends on real LLM output quality and semantic understanding. Mocking the LLM cannot reveal whether the actual model generates off-topic responses for a given question. Testing requires real LLM calls to detect non-deterministic failures in response relevance across different prompts and customer scenarios.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real LLM with specific customer questions (e.g., 'How do I reset my password?', 'What is your refund policy?') and conversation history. Verify the response contains customer-specific details or question markers that confirm the question was addressed, not a generic template. Parametrize test across at least 5 diverse customer scenarios (product inquiry, billing, technical support, account issue, etc.).

behaviors.description: The tone-checking classifier correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers response regeneration
behaviors.justification: The tone classifier is most likely LLM-based (required for semantic tone detection). Real LLM output for tone classification can vary, especially for borderline cases. To catch the failure of 'formal response not flagged for regeneration', we need real tone classifier calls with real or carefully-crafted test responses. If the classifier is deterministic/rule-based, Integration level would suffice; if LLM-based (expected), Agentic is needed.
behaviors.test_description: Generate a formal-tone response through the real LLM. Invoke the tone classifier on this response with a casual-tier customer. Assert regeneration is triggered. Repeat with edge-case responses (borderline formal/casual boundary cases, corporate jargon) to catch misclassifications. Include at least 2 customer tiers (casual, formal) with matching/mismatching tone responses.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Assumes tone classifier is LLM-based for semantic understanding; if implementation is rule-based (keyword/sentiment), Integration level would be sufficient. Confidence split reflects this uncertainty.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, and any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps and no data loss
behaviors.justification: A unit test with mocked database cannot catch real persistence failures: schema mismatches, missing fields, incorrect timestamp values, incomplete storage of regenerated responses, or race conditions in multi-write scenarios. We must test against a real or in-memory test database that validates schema compliance and data integrity.
behaviors.test_description: Use an in-memory test database. Invoke the chatbot with a user message, generate a response, and trigger tone-based regeneration. Query the database and assert: (1) all messages and responses are stored, (2) regeneration history is preserved, (3) timestamps are present and time-ordered, (4) no data is truncated or lost. Verify both the database record and returned API response contain the complete exchange.

behaviors.description: When tone regeneration is triggered, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft
behaviors.justification: The failure is the customer receiving or accessing a rejected draft. Mocking the database alone cannot verify this; we need integration testing of the replacement logic that ensures only the final response is prepared for customer delivery. The test must verify both the stored response and the prepared customer response exclude the rejected draft.
behaviors.test_description: Trigger tone-based regeneration by forcing a formal response for a casual-tier customer. Verify: (1) only the regenerated response is returned to the customer API/endpoint, (2) the database contains only the regenerated response in the active/delivered field, (3) the rejected initial response is not accessible to the customer. Inspect response content to confirm it matches the regenerated version, not the rejected one.
